result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant EncReg as enc_keys registration
    participant Server
    participant Initiator
    participant Responder

    alt secretA trace
        Attacker->>Server: (B, B)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((B,spk(sskB)),skS), sign((B,pk(skB)),skS)
        Attacker->>EncReg: (B, pk(y))
        Attacker->>Server: (a2, B)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((a2,a4),skS), sign((B,pk(y)),skS)
        Attacker->>Initiator: xA = B
        Attacker->>Initiator: cert1 = sign((B,spk(sskB)),skS), cert2 = sign((B,pk(y)),skS)
        Initiator-->>Attacker: cert1, cert2, encrypt(sign((B,B,k),sskB), pk(y))
        Initiator-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretA, k)
        Note over Attacker: Trace shows attacker uses y to recover k and then decrypts secretA
    else secretB trace
        Attacker->>EncReg: (A, spk(a9))
        Attacker->>Server: (a1, A)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((a1,a5),skS), sign((A,spk(a9)),skS)
        Attacker->>Server: (a3, B)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((a3,a7),skS), sign((B,pk(skB)),skS)
        Attacker->>Responder: xB = B
        Attacker->>Responder: cert1 = sign((A,spk(a9)),skS), cert2 = sign((B,pk(skB)),skS), encrypt(sign((A,B,a11),a9), pk(skB))
        Responder-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretB, a11)
        Note over Attacker: Trace shows attacker knows a11 and decrypts secretB
    end
result.explanation: Both secrecy queries fail in the provided run. The common issue shown by the traces is that process_encK accepts attacker-supplied encryption-key registrations for any host, including honest A and B. The server then signs those table entries. In the secretA trace, the attacker gets a server-signed certificate for B with attacker-controlled key pk(y), starts the initiator with xA = B, receives encrypt(sign((B,B,k),sskB), pk(y)), recovers k, and then decrypts sencrypt(secretA,k). In the secretB trace, the attacker registers (A, spk(a9)) through the encryption-key path, obtains sign((A,spk(a9)),skS) from the server, and reuses that signed value as cert1; the responder accepts it and outputs sencrypt(secretB,a11), which the attacker decrypts. So the model lets attacker-chosen keys be certified for honest identities, and the second trace also shows that an encryption-key certificate can be reused where a signature-key certificate is expected.
result.notes: The provided evidence clearly shows secrecy breaks for secretA and secretB. It does not show whether the one-line process_encK fix also resolves the remaining injective correspondence failures.
